directions.name: Apply Only A Minimal PTX Export Address Cleanup
directions.hypothesis: Round 6/17 kept correctness but still regressed to 25.77151966 ms, so the grouped-row window retune is now closed alongside the earlier prefetch and expanded B-shared-skew attempts. That leaves the narrowest surviving family on top of the accepted PTX base: a minimal export-address cleanup that does not reopen scratch lifetime, traversal direction, or shared-layout risk. Round 2/17 already showed that modest export cleanup can help, so the next best move is to trim only the remaining address setup in the PTX store helpers.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual address-generation and store-helper overhead in the surviving PTX export path.
directions.risk: Low. The expected upside is smaller than a broader path switch, but it stays closest to the last accepted correct baseline and avoids reopening already closed families.
directions.stop_condition: Stop after one bounded cleanup if it only produces noise-level movement or fails to beat 25.50532818 ms.

directions.name: Reopen The Measured 64x384 Fixed-Main-Tile Control Path
directions.hypothesis: The surviving PTX-adjacent tweaks are narrowing, and the older fixed-main-tile sweep is still the only alternate family in the current context with measured ranking data: `64x384` was the best tile in that sweep. It was measured on an older baseline, so it should not outrank the narrow PTX cleanup, but it remains a legitimate control family if the PTX microkernel has plateaued and the broader 64x384 hot-band path has become competitive again on the current code base.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Broader hot-band path selection and arithmetic-intensity tradeoff rather than PTX microkernel control overhead.
directions.risk: Medium. This family has direct measured ancestry, but it is also broader than the accepted PTX base and can easily give back the gains won in later rounds.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if the promoted 64x384 control path is clearly slower than the accepted PTX baseline or reopens the broad-family regressions that were already beaten.

directions.name: Use The Non-PTX 128x128 Sibling As A Control
directions.hypothesis: If the remaining overhead is specific to the PTX export/store path, the non-PTX 128x128 sibling should expose that with a simpler store path and a more stable correctness surface than another shared-layout rewrite. It is ranked behind the export cleanup and the 64x384 control because it has less direct measured support in the current loop, but it remains a distinct fallback family once the PTX-adjacent moves stall.
directions.expected_bottleneck: PTX-specific export/store complexity versus the simpler non-PTX 128x128 sibling.
directions.risk: Low to medium. The path should stay bounded and correct more easily than another orchestration or shared-layout rewrite, but it may simply underperform the accepted PTX baseline.
directions.stop_condition: Use this only if the higher-ranked directions stall; close it quickly if it is both slower and no cleaner in the profiler.

notes: Round 7/17 diagnosis for run 20260420_160231_bf16_gemm_v1_8ed8cf3. Human-review mapping for this round: keep broad retile/default-promotion reopening low priority, and keep the immediate prefetch-handoff, expanded B-shared-skew, and grouped-row-window families closed. Round 5 proved the multi-gap B-shared layout was invalid because correctness failed 3/3, and round 6 proved the grouped-row retune stayed correct but still regressed to 25.77151966 ms with long-scoreboard rising to 7.83 and DRAM nudging up to 11.74. No new explicit human idea family is queued in state/human_review.md, so the surviving ranking is: accept one last minimal PTX export-address cleanup as the primary narrow move on top of the accepted base 20260420_154827_bf16_gemm_v1_7adfc4e at 25.50532818 ms; defer the historically measured 64x384 fixed-main-tile control because its evidence is older and broader; keep the non-PTX 128x128 sibling only as a fallback control.
